 {"id":"wa-6sk.3","title":"Implement `wa timeline` command: ASCII timeline with correlations","description":"# `wa timeline` command (ASCII timeline)

## Purpose
Display a unified event timeline in the terminal with an ASCII visualization and optional correlation markers.

## Usage
```bash
wa timeline [--last <duration>] [--pane <id>] [--type <event-type>] [--format {auto|plain|json}] [-v|-vv]
```

Notes:
- `--format` follows the global human CLI output convention (`wa-nu4.3.2.1`).
- `-v/-vv` controls how much detail is shown (event fields, correlation reasoning, etc.).

## Output format (plain example)
```
Timeline: 2026-01-18 14:00 - 14:30 (4 panes, 12 events)

14:02:15 ─┬─ Pane 1 (codex): session.started
          │
14:05:32 ─┼─ Pane 3 (claude): session.started
          │
14:12:01 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): session.compaction ──→ handled (14:12:08)
          │
14:28:03 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): codex.usage_limit_reached
          ├──→ workflow: handle_usage_limits (in progress)
          │
14:28:45 ─┴─ Pane 7 (codex-backup): session.started [CORRELATED: failover]

Legend: ──→ workflow action  [CORRELATED] cross-pane relationship
```

## Features
- Vertical timeline with timestamps.
- Events grouped by timestamp proximity.
- Workflow actions shown with arrows.
- Correlations highlighted with labels.
- Color coding in `--format auto` when stderr/stdout is a TTY.
- Legend for symbols.

## Options
- `--last`: Time range (e.g., `30m`, `2h`, `1d`).
- `--pane`: Filter to specific pane(s).
- `--type`: Filter to specific event types.
- `--format json`: Machine-readable output (schema-stable).
- `--format plain`: No ANSI output (stable for piping/snapshots).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timeline displays events chronologically.
- [ ] Correlations are visually indicated.
- [ ] Handled events show workflow info.
- [ ] Filtering works correctly.
- [ ] JSON output is complete and schema-valid.

## Testing
- Unit tests for correlation heuristics and aggregation.
- Integration tests for timeline queries and rendering.
- E2E scenario verifies correlations with artifacts (see `wa-ugg`).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:50:50.689751477Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:47:22.176011348Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-cli","area-ux","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-6sk.3","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:02Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-6sk.3","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:02Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-6sk.3","depends_on_id":"wa-6sk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:02Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-6sk.3","depends_on_id":"wa-6sk.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:02Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-6sk.3","depends_on_id":"wa-6sk.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:02Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-6sk.4","title":"TUI timeline view: interactive scrollable timeline with zoom","description":"
# TUI Timeline View

## Purpose
Provide an interactive timeline visualization in the TUI dashboard.

## UI Design
```
┌─ Timeline ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 14:00                    14:15                    14:30        [zoom]  │
│   │                        │                        │                  │
│ P1├─●session.started───────┼─●compaction──────────●usage_limit────────│
│   │                        │  └→handled            └→workflow         │
│ P3├────────────●session.started──────────────────────────────────────│
│   │                        │                        │                  │
│ P7├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────●session[failover]│
│   │                        │                        │                  │
│   │←───────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼─────────────────→│
│                    ◀ [zoom out]  [zoom in] ▶                          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
[←/→] scroll  [+/-] zoom  [Enter] select  [f] filter  [?] help
```

## Features
1. Horizontal timeline axis
2. Vertical lanes per pane
3. Event dots with labels
4. Correlation lines connecting events
5. Zoom in/out with keyboard
6. Scroll left/right in time
7. Click events to see details panel

## Implementation
- Ratatui canvas widget for timeline drawing
- Efficient rendering: only visible events
- Smooth scrolling and zooming
- Event details popup on selection

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timeline renders with proper proportions
- [ ] Zoom levels work (1m to 24h range)
- [ ] Scroll is smooth
- [ ] Event selection shows details
- [ ] Correlations drawn as connecting lines

## Testing
- Unit tests for correlation heuristics and aggregation.
- Integration tests for timeline queries and rendering.
- E2E scenario verifies correlations with artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-6sk.5","title":"Timeline tests: correlation detection, query performance, rendering","description":"
# Timeline Testing Suite

## Purpose
Ensure timeline functionality works correctly and performs well.

## Test Categories

### 1. Correlation Detection Tests
- Failover detection: usage limit + new session = correlated
- Temporal clustering: events within window grouped
- Workflow groups: events linked to same workflow
- False positive tests: unrelated events not correlated

### 2. Query Performance Tests
- Large time ranges: 1000+ events in <100ms
- Index usage verification
- Pagination works correctly

### 3. Rendering Tests
- ASCII timeline output snapshots
- Wide/narrow terminal handling
- Color and no-color modes
- JSON output completeness

### 4. Edge Cases
- Empty timeline (no events)
- Single event
- Events at exact same timestamp
- Very long event descriptions

## Test Fixtures
- Standard scenarios with known correlations
- Performance benchmark dataset (10k events)
- Visual regression snapshots

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correlation detection >90% accuracy
- [ ] Query time <100ms for typical ranges
- [ ] Output snapshots stable
- [ ] Edge cases handled gracefully

## Testing
- Unit tests for correlation heuristics and aggregation.
- Integration tests for timeline queries and rendering.
- E2E scenario verifies correlations with artifacts.

-{"id":"wa-7dd","title":"wa robot workflow status: check progress of running workflows","description":"# wa robot workflow status

## Purpose
Allow agents to query the status of running or completed workflows to enable monitoring, chaining, and error handling.

## Command Interface
```bash
# Check status of a specific workflow by execution ID
wa robot workflow status <execution_id>

# Check all workflows on a pane
wa robot workflow status --pane 3

# Check all active workflows
wa robot workflow status --active

# Include step logs
wa robot workflow status <execution_id> --verbose
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.status",
  "data": {
    "execution_id": "wf-abc123",
    "workflow_name": "handle_compaction",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "trigger_event_id": 42,
    "status": "running",
    "current_step": 2,
    "total_steps": 5,
    "step_name": "wait_for_prompt",
    "elapsed_ms": 5234,
    "last_step_result": "continue",
    "started_at": 1737200000000,
    "updated_at": 1737205234000,
    "completed_at": null,
    "step_logs": [
      {
        "step_index": 0,
        "step_name": "check_context",
        "result_type": "continue",
        "started_at": 1737200000000,
        "completed_at": 1737200100000,
        "duration_ms": 100
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Status Values
- `pending` - Workflow queued but not started
- `running` - Actively executing steps
- `waiting` - Waiting for condition (pattern, idle, etc.)
- `paused` - Paused (e.g., awaiting approval)
- `completed` - Successfully finished all steps
- `failed` - Encountered error
- `aborted` - Manually aborted

## Error Cases (Stable Codes)
- E_EXECUTION_NOT_FOUND: No workflow execution with given ID
- E_PANE_NOT_FOUND: No such pane (when using --pane)

## Use Cases
1. **Progress monitoring**: Agent polls status while waiting for long workflow
2. **Error recovery**: Check why workflow failed before retrying
3. **Chaining**: Wait for workflow completion before starting dependent task
4. **Debugging**: Understand current step when things are slow

## Implementation Notes
- Query workflow_executions + workflow_step_log tables
- Include timing for each completed step
- Redact sensitive data in step results
- Support --verbose for full step logs

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_status_running_workflow() {
    let exec_id = start_test_workflow();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", &exec_id]);
    
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert_eq!(output["data"]["status"], "running");
    assert!(output["data"]["current_step"].is_number());
    assert!(output["data"]["elapsed_ms"].is_number());
}

#[test]
fn test_status_completed_workflow() {
    let exec_id = run_workflow_to_completion();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", &exec_id]);
    
    assert_eq!(output["data"]["status"], "completed");
    assert!(output["data"]["completed_at"].is_number());
}

#[test]
fn test_status_not_found_error() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", "nonexistent-id"]);
    
    assert!(!output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_EXECUTION_NOT_FOUND");
}

#[test]
fn test_status_by_pane() {
    let exec_id = start_workflow_on_pane(3);
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", "--pane", "3"]);
    
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    let executions = output["data"]["executions"].as_array().unwrap();
    assert!(executions.iter().any(|e| e["execution_id"] == exec_id));
}

#[test]
fn test_status_verbose_includes_step_logs() {
    let exec_id = start_test_workflow();
    advance_workflow_steps(2);
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", &exec_id, "--verbose"]);
    
    let step_logs = output["data"]["step_logs"].as_array().unwrap();
    assert!(step_logs.len() >= 2);
    assert!(step_logs[0]["duration_ms"].is_number());
}

#[test]
fn test_status_json_schema_validation() {
    let exec_id = start_test_workflow();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", &exec_id]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-status.json");
}
```

### E2E Test
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_workflow_status.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR:-/tmp}/workflow_status.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Workflow Status E2E ==="

# 1. Start a workflow
log "Starting test workflow..."
RUN=$(wa robot workflow run handle_compaction 0 --dry-run 2>&1 || true)
log "Run result: $RUN"

EXEC_ID=$(echo "$RUN" | jq -r '.data.execution_id // empty')
if [ -z "$EXEC_ID" ]; then
    log "No execution ID returned, using mock"
    EXEC_ID="test-exec-123"
fi

# 2. Check status
log "Checking status for $EXEC_ID..."
STATUS=$(wa robot workflow status "$EXEC_ID" 2>&1 || true)
log "Status: $STATUS"

# 3. Check for expected fields
if echo "$STATUS" | jq -e '.ok == true' > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "$STATUS" | jq -e '.data.status' || { log "FAIL: no status field"; exit 1; }
    echo "$STATUS" | jq -e '.data.workflow_name' || { log "FAIL: no workflow_name"; exit 1; }
    log "Status check passed"
else
    # Execution not found is expected for dry-run
    echo "$STATUS" | jq -e '.error.code == "E_EXECUTION_NOT_FOUND"' || { log "FAIL: unexpected error"; exit 1; }
    log "Got expected not-found error"
fi

# 4. Test --active flag
log "Testing --active flag..."
ACTIVE=$(wa robot workflow status --active 2>&1 || true)
log "Active: $ACTIVE"
echo "$ACTIVE" | jq -e '.ok' || { log "FAIL: active query failed"; exit 1; }

log "=== PASS: workflow_status ==="
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Status query returns accurate real-time progress
- [ ] All status values correctly represented
- [ ] Step-level detail available (name, timing)
- [ ] --pane filter works
- [ ] --active filter works
- [ ] --verbose includes step logs
- [ ] JSON validates against wa-robot-workflow-status.json schema
- [ ] Error cases return stable E_* codes
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] E2E test passes with detailed logging

 {"id":"wa-7fv","title":"E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)","description":"# E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that backup/restore is safe, user-friendly, and reliable:
- export produces a self-contained, verifiable backup artifact
- import rejects corrupt backups with actionable errors
- replace/merge semantics behave correctly
- pre-import safety backups are created when mutating an existing workspace
- no secrets leak into logs/artifacts

This is the system-level validation for `wa-1wg`.

## Key constraints
- Must follow the standard E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and run via the standard runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- **No destructive shell cleanup** in test scripts (no `rm -rf`).
  - Use fresh temporary workspaces/directories per scenario to reset state.
- Deterministic synchronization:
  - no fixed sleeps as the primary sync primitive
  - wait on explicit conditions (segments/events present, watcher stopped, etc.) with bounded timeouts

## Test setup (harness)
- Use a dedicated E2E workspace root for this case.
- Each scenario uses its own fresh workspace dir:
  - `workspace_a/`, `workspace_b/`, …
- Generate minimal deterministic data:
  - spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token and/or emits a known event
  - run `wa watch` long enough to persist at least one segment
  - stop watcher cleanly (`wa stop --workspace …` or SIGINT with bounded wait)

## Scenarios

### 1) Clean export/import round-trip
**Intent:** exported backup round-trips core data.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_a` with known captured data (at least one segment + one searchable token).
2. Export:
   - `wa backup export --workspace workspace_a --output artifacts/backup.wa`
3. Record a minimal evidence snapshot from `workspace_a` (stable fields only):
   - segment count (or “token is searchable” evidence)
   - event count (if applicable)
4. Import into a fresh `workspace_b`:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup.wa --replace --yes`
5. Verify `workspace_b` matches the recorded evidence (stable fields only).

Assertions:
- backup file exists and is verifiable (`--verify` or checksum metadata).
- restored workspace contains the expected token/search hit.

### 2) Corrupt backup rejection
**Intent:** corruption is detected and rejected.

Steps:
1. Create a valid backup.
2. Corrupt it by appending bytes:
   - `printf 'garbage' >> artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa`
3. Import must fail:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa …` (expected non-zero)

Assertions:
- error mentions integrity verification (checksum/manifest) and provides remediation.
- no partial restore occurs (workspace remains empty or unchanged).

### 3) Pre-import safety backup is created
**Intent:** importing into an existing workspace never destroys data without preserving a recovery artifact.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_c` with some data.
2. Export an “external” backup from `workspace_a` (or another workspace).
3. Import into `workspace_c` with replace mode:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_c artifacts/external.wa --replace --yes`

Assertions:
- A pre-import backup file is created in the configured backup destination for `workspace_c`.
- The pre-import backup is itself verifiable.

### 4) Metadata-only backup
**Intent:** metadata-only export exists and is meaningfully smaller.

Steps:
1. Export full backup and metadata-only backup.
2. Compare file sizes.

Assertions:
- metadata-only file is smaller by a meaningful factor (threshold chosen to avoid flakiness).
- metadata-only backup still contains manifest/version info.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `backup.wa`, `backup_corrupt.wa`, `external.wa`
- `doctor_workspace_paths.txt` (workspace roots + db paths)
- `roundtrip_evidence.json` (stable-field evidence)
- `restore_verify.json` (verification results)
- `backup_restore_e2e.log` (timestamped steps + durations)

## Logging contract
- Every step logs:
  - scenario name
  - workspace path
  - command invoked (redacted)
  - pass/fail with durations
- On failure, print artifact pointers and a short summary.

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps; waits are explicit and bounded
- Safety:
  - scan all artifacts/logs for secret-like strings (reuse the redaction scan approach from `wa-4vx.10.18`)
  - ensure backups do not embed secrets in clear (especially config)
- Correctness:
  - verify round-trip evidence (token searchable; stable counts)
  - verify corruption rejection and no partial restore

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Round-trip export/import preserves core data.
- [ ] Corrupt backups are rejected with clear errors.
- [ ] Pre-import safety backup is created and verifiable.
- [ ] Metadata-only export exists and is smaller.

 {"id":"wa-7wk","title":"Decision context capture: PolicyEngine records full reasoning for every decision","description":"# Task: Decision Context Capture

## Goal
Modify PolicyEngine and workflow runner to capture **full decision context** (not just the result) for every policy decision.

## Why This Matters
To explain "why" a decision was made, we need to know:
- What conditions were evaluated
- Which rules matched or did not match
- What evidence was considered
- How the final decision was reached

Without this context, `wa why` cannot provide meaningful explanations.

## Current State
PolicyEngine returns `PolicyDecision`:
```rust
enum PolicyDecision {
    Allow,
    Deny { reason: String },
    RequireApproval { code: String },
}
```

This is insufficient for explanation—we know the result but not the reasoning.

## Target State
PolicyEngine returns `PolicyDecision` with full context:
```rust
struct PolicyDecision {
    result: PolicyResult,
    context: DecisionContext,
}

struct DecisionContext {
    /// Timestamp of decision
    timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    
    /// Action being evaluated
    action: ActionKind,
    
    /// Target of action
    target: ActionTarget,
    
    /// Capabilities observed
    capabilities: PaneCapabilities,
    
    /// Rules evaluated, in order
    rules_evaluated: Vec<RuleEvaluation>,
    
    /// The rule that determined the outcome (if any)
    determining_rule: Option<RuleId>,
    
    /// Evidence collected during evaluation
    evidence: Vec<Evidence>,
    
    /// Rate limit state at decision time
    rate_limit_state: Option<RateLimitState>,
}

struct RuleEvaluation {
    rule_id: RuleId,
    matched: bool,
    reason: Option<String>,
}

enum Evidence {
    PaneState { key: String, value: String },
    Timestamp { key: String, value: DateTime<Utc> },
    Flag { key: String, value: bool },
}
```

## Implementation

### 1. Capture During Evaluation
```rust
impl PolicyEngine {
    pub fn authorize(&self, action: &Action) -> PolicyDecision {
        let mut context = DecisionContext::new(action);
        
        // Capture capabilities
        context.capabilities = self.derive_capabilities(action.pane_id);
        
        // Evaluate rules, recording each
        for rule in &self.rules {
            let evaluation = rule.evaluate(&context.capabilities);
            context.rules_evaluated.push(evaluation.clone());
            
            if evaluation.matched && evaluation.is_terminal() {
                context.determining_rule = Some(rule.id.clone());
                break;
            }
        }
        
        // Build decision with context
        PolicyDecision {
            result: self.compute_result(&context),
            context,
        }
    }
}
```

### 2. Store in Audit Trail
```sql
-- Extend audit_actions table
ALTER TABLE audit_actions ADD COLUMN decision_context BLOB;
-- Store as MessagePack for efficiency, or JSON for debuggability
```

### 3. Query Interface
```rust
impl StorageHandle {
    pub fn get_decision_context(
        &self,
        decision_id: &str,
    ) -> Result<DecisionContext, Error>;
    
    pub fn get_recent_decisions(
        &self,
        filter: DecisionFilter,
        limit: usize,
    ) -> Result<Vec<(Decision, DecisionContext)>, Error>;
}
```

## Storage Considerations
- Context can be large; consider compression
- Retention policy: keep context for N days, then prune
- Index by pane_id, action_type for efficient queries

## Testing
- Unit tests: Context is captured correctly for various scenarios
- Integration tests: Context survives storage roundtrip
- Query tests: Filtering by pane/action works correctly

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every PolicyDecision includes full DecisionContext
- Context is persisted in audit trail
- Context can be queried by decision_id, pane_id, action_type
- Context is sufficient for `wa why` to generate explanations

 {"id":"wa-8bk","title":"[EPIC] UX Excellence: Explainability, Ergonomics, and User Delight","description":"# [EPIC] UX Excellence: Explainability, Ergonomics, and User Delight

## Mission
Transform wa from a capable but opaque automation tool into a **transparent, delightful, and trustworthy** system that users love to use.

## Why This Matters
Terminal automation is inherently scary. Users cannot see what wa is doing, and when things go wrong, they often do not understand why. This creates:
- **Fear**: "What if it sends to the wrong pane?"
- **Frustration**: "Why did it deny my send?"
- **Distrust**: "I do not know what it is doing"

Great UX transforms these into:
- **Confidence**: "I can preview before executing"
- **Understanding**: "I know exactly why it made that decision"
- **Trust**: "I can see everything happening in real-time"

## Core Insight: Legibility
The unifying theme across all these improvements is **legibility**—making wa's behavior visible and understandable:
1. **Legible decisions** → Deep Explainability (`wa why` + enriched errors)
2. **Legible state** → Interactive TUI Dashboard
3. **Legible intentions** → Dry-run preview mode
4. **Legible history** → Event timeline with correlation
5. **Legible next steps** → Quick-fix suggestions

## Scope (30 Improvements)
This epic encompasses improvements across multiple dimensions:

### Reliability & Robustness (5)
- Watchdog/heartbeat with auto-restart
- Circuit breaker for failed services
- Chaos testing harness
- Retry with exponential backoff
- Graceful degradation modes

### Performance (5)
- Lazy pattern compilation
- Bloom filter pre-filter
- Connection pooling for WezTerm CLI
- Background compression
- Incremental FTS sync

### User Experience & Ergonomics (5)
- Interactive TUI dashboard (expand existing)
- Shell completions for all commands
- Progressive disclosure in output
- Smart aliases
- Natural language event descriptions

### Intuitiveness & Discoverability (5)
- `wa why` command for decision explanation
- `wa learn` interactive tutorial
- Contextual help in every error
- Smart defaults based on environment detection
- In-CLI documentation

### Usefulness & Compelling Features (5)
- Session recording and replay
- Event timeline with cross-pane correlation
- Notification webhooks (Slack/Discord/email)
- Export to markdown/HTML
- Proactive suggestions ("Account X at 85%")

### Safety & Trust (5)
- Dry-run for all human commands
- Confirmation prompts with policy reason
- Rollback log visualization
- Simulation mode (mock WezTerm)
- Plugin/extension system for custom patterns

## Success Criteria
- New users can understand wa's behavior within 10 minutes
- Errors are self-explanatory and include actionable fixes
- Users can preview any action before executing
- The TUI provides real-time visibility into all operations
- Support burden decreases because users can self-serve

## Testing
Each sub-feature has its own testing requirements. At the epic level:
- User testing with 3+ external developers (collect feedback)
- Measure time-to-first-success for new users
- Track error resolution time (should decrease)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 30 improvement areas have implemented beads with tests
- Documentation covers all new commands and features
- TUI is polished and accessible (keyboard navigation, screen reader support)
- Error messages meet the "actionable fix included" standard

 {"id":"wa-rnf.3","title":"Progressive disclosure: default/verbose/debug output levels","description":"# Progressive disclosure (verbosity)

## Goal
Show essential information by default, with increasing detail available on request, without sacrificing scriptability.

## Key design rule
- `-v/-vv` controls **how much** information we include.
- `--format {auto|plain|json}` controls **how** we render it.

This keeps CLI behavior consistent and avoids a proliferation of ad-hoc `--json` flags.

## Verbosity levels
- Default (no flag): Essential info only
- `-v`: Additional context
- `-vv`: Debug details

## JSON mode
- Use `--format json` for machine-readable output.
- JSON output must be schema-stable and compatible with non-TTY usage.

## Example: `wa status`
```bash
# Default
$ wa status
Panes: 4 observed, 1 ignored
Events: 2 unhandled

# Verbose
$ wa status -v
Panes:
  1: codex [PromptActive] /home/user/project (last: 2m ago)
  3: claude [CommandRunning] /home/user/other (last: 5s ago)
  5: local [Ignored] (exclude rule: title=htop)
  7: gemini [PromptActive] /home/user/gemini (last: 30s ago)

Events (unhandled):
  - codex.usage_limit_reached (Pane 1, 2m ago)
  - session.compaction (Pane 3, 5m ago)

# Debug
$ wa status -vv
[as above, plus:]
Health:
  Queue depth: 0
  Ingest lag: 12ms
  DB size: 1.2GB
  Circuit breakers: all closed

# Machine
$ wa status --format json | jq .
```

## Implementation sketch
```rust
#[derive(Parser)]
struct Cli {
    #[arg(short, long, action = ArgAction::Count)]
    verbose: u8,
}

fn format_status(status: &Status, verbosity: u8) -> String {
    match verbosity {
        0 => format_status_brief(status),
        1 => format_status_verbose(status),
        _ => format_status_debug(status),
    }
}
```

## Consistency rules
- All human commands support `-v` and `-vv`.
- Default shows what the user needs to know.
- Verbose adds context for understanding.
- Debug adds internal state for troubleshooting.
- `--format json` output must not contain ANSI escapes and must be schema-stable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All commands support `-v/-vv`.
- [ ] Default output is clean and minimal.
- [ ] Verbose adds useful context.
- [ ] Debug shows internal state.
- [ ] Consistent pattern across all commands.

## Testing
- Unit tests for verbosity routing (brief/verbose/debug paths).
- Contract tests:
  - `--format json` schema validation
  - no ANSI escapes in non-TTY/plain/json
- Help/output snapshot tests where appropriate (normalized).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LilacMeadow","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:55:23.666085891Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T04:53:54.213508086Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T04:53:54.213378616Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-cli","area-ux","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-rnf.3","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.2.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:08Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-rnf.3","depends_on_id":"wa-rnf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:08Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":101,"issue_id":"wa-rnf.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progressive disclosure implemented: changed Cli.verbose from bool to u8 with ArgAction::Count (-v, -vv), updated RenderContext.verbose from bool to u8, added is_verbose()/is_debug() convenience methods.  {"id":"wa-rnf.4","title":"Consistent help patterns: examples, cross-references, unified style","description":"
# Consistent Help Patterns

## Purpose
Make help text comprehensive, consistent, and genuinely helpful.

## Help Structure
Every command has:
1. One-line description
2. Usage synopsis
3. Options with descriptions
4. Examples (most important!)
5. See Also (related commands)

## Template
```
wa <command> - <one-line description>

USAGE:
    wa <command> [OPTIONS] [ARGS]

OPTIONS:
    -o, --option <VALUE>  Description [default: X]

EXAMPLES:
    wa <command> arg              Basic usage
    wa <command> --opt val arg    With option
    wa <command> --dry-run arg    Preview mode

SEE ALSO:
    wa related-cmd    Related functionality
    wa another        Alternative approach
```

## Implementation
```rust
#[derive(Parser)]
#[command(
    about = "Send text to a pane",
    long_about = None,
    after_help = SEND_EXAMPLES,
)]
struct SendCmd { ... }

const SEND_EXAMPLES: &str = r#"
EXAMPLES:
    wa send --pane 3 "hello"
    wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello"
    wa send --pane 3 --wait-for ">>>" "continue"

SEE ALSO:
    wa status       List available panes
    wa why          Explain send failures
"#;
```

## Validation
CI check ensures:
- All commands have help text
- All commands have examples
- No broken cross-references

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All commands follow consistent help template
- [ ] All commands have at least 2 examples
- [ ] See Also references are accurate
- [ ] CI validates help text completeness

## Testing
- Unit tests for completion/alias generation and help formatting.
- Integration tests for help output snapshots and no-ANSI guarantees.
- CI smoke tests for completions and help stability.

 {"id":"wa-rnf.5","title":"CLI polish tests: completions, aliases, verbosity, help validation","description":"# CLI polish tests (completions, aliases, verbosity, help)

## Goal
Ensure CLI polish features work correctly and consistently, and remain regression-resistant as new commands are added.

## Test categories

### 1) Completion tests
- Static completions generated for all shells.
- Dynamic completions return correct pane IDs and workflow names.
- No duplicate or missing completions.

### 2) Alias tests
- Built-in aliases expand correctly.
- User aliases load from config.
- Alias conflicts detected.
- `wa aliases` output is accurate.

### 3) Verbosity + format tests
- Default output matches expected format.
- `-v` adds expected additional info.
- `-vv` adds debug-level info.
- `--format json` produces valid, schema-stable JSON.

### 4) Help validation
- All commands have help text.
- All commands have examples.
- Cross-references point to real commands.
- No obvious typos in help text.

### 5) Output consistency
- No ANSI when stdout is not a TTY.
- Exit codes are correct (0 success, 1 error).
- Error messages include remediation.
- JSON envelope is consistent across commands.

## Snapshot/contract tests
- Help text snapshots for regression (normalized).
- Status output snapshots at each verbosity level (normalized; stable ordering).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Completion tests for all shells.
- [ ] Alias expansion tests.
- [ ] Verbosity/format tests.
- [ ] Help text validation in CI.
- [ ] Output consistency verified.

- Unit: completion generation + alias expansion + verbosity routing.

-{"id":"wa-smm","title":"Workflow: handle_gemini_quota (Gemini API quota management)","description":"# Workflow: handle_gemini_quota

## Purpose
Handle Gemini-specific quota and rate limit scenarios:
- Daily API quota limits
- Requests-per-minute rate limits
- Model-specific token limits

## Detection Patterns
```
# Quota exhausted
"Resource has been exhausted"
"quota exceeded"
"RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED"

# Rate limit
"Too many requests"
"Please slow down"
"429 Too Many Requests"

# Token limit
"maximum token limit"
"context length exceeded"
```

## Workflow Steps

### Step 1: Detect and classify
- Parse error code/message
- Determine: quota vs rate vs token limit
- Extract reset time if available

### Step 2: Quota exhaustion handling
1. Check if alternate API key available
2. If yes: rotate to new key
3. If no: notify user with reset time
4. Persist state for resume at reset

### Step 3: Rate limit handling
1. Extract retry-after header/message
2. Implement exponential backoff
3. Resume when safe

### Step 4: Token limit handling
1. Similar to Claude Code context handling
2. Suggest conversation restart
3. Save state for continuity

## Safety Constraints
- Don't spam retries
- Respect API terms of service
- Preserve conversation state

## Configuration
```toml
[workflows.handle_gemini_quota]
api_key_rotation = false  # Require explicit enable
max_retries = 3
backoff_base_seconds = 60
```

## Testing
- Fixture: quota error patterns
- Fixture: rate limit error patterns
- Unit: backoff calculation
- E2E: quota → notification flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All Gemini quota types detected
- [ ] Rate limits handled with backoff
- [ ] API key rotation works when enabled
- [ ] User notified with reset times","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:13:38.597646095Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:51:26.910509302Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-workflows","phase-3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-smm","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:09Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-smm","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:09Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-smm","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:09Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-smm","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-spi","title":"Watch-and-notify mode: wa watch --notify-only for alert-only monitoring","description":"# Watch-and-Notify Mode: Continuous monitoring with alert delivery

## Purpose
Enable wa to run in notification-only mode, alerting on events without auto-handling.

## Use Case
Some users want to be notified of events but handle them manually:
- Learning the system behavior
- High-stakes environments requiring human review
- Debugging automation issues

## Implementation

### CLI Command
```bash
# Watch and notify on all events
$ wa watch --notify-only

# Watch and notify only on specific events
$ wa watch --notify-only --notify-filter "usage_limit,compaction"

# Watch with specific notification channels
$ wa watch --notify-only --notify-via webhook,desktop
```

### Configuration
```toml
[notifications]
# Enable watch-and-notify mode
notify_only = true

# Which events trigger notifications
notify_filter = ["*.usage_limit*", "*.compaction", "*.error"]

# Notification channels
channels = ["webhook", "desktop"]

# Throttling
min_interval_seconds = 60
max_per_hour = 30
```

### NotifyOnlyMode Implementation
```rust
pub struct NotifyOnlyWatcher {
    event_bus: EventBus,
    notifier: Notifier,
    filter: EventFilter,
    throttle: ThrottleState,
}

impl NotifyOnlyWatcher {
    pub async fn run(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        loop {
            let event = self.event_bus.recv().await?;

            if !self.filter.matches(&event) {
                continue;
            }

            if !self.throttle.allow(&event) {
                tracing::debug!("Throttled notification for {:?}", event.rule_id);
                continue;
            }

            // Log event without handling
            tracing::info!(
                event_id = %event.id,
                rule_id = %event.rule_id,
                pane_id = %event.pane_id,
                "Event detected (notify-only mode)"
            );

            // Send notification
            self.notifier.send(Notification {
                title: format!("wa: {}", event.rule_id),
                body: event.to_notification_body(),
                severity: event.severity(),
                actions: event.suggested_actions(),
            }).await?;
        }
    }
}
```

### Notification Body Generation
```rust
impl Event {
    pub fn to_notification_body(&self) -> String {
        format!(
            "Detected: {}\nPane: {} ({})\nTime: {}\n\nSuggested action:\n  {}",
            self.rule_id,
            self.pane_id,
            self.pane_title,
            self.timestamp.format("%H:%M:%S"),
            self.suggested_action().unwrap_or("No action suggested"),
        )
    }
}
```

### Desktop Notification Example
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 🔔 wa: codex.usage_limit_reached    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Pane 9 (codex @ /project)           │
│ Detected: 14:30:15                  │
│                                     │
│ Run: wa workflow run handle_usage.. │
│                                     │
│ [Dismiss]  [Handle]  [View Details] │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Testing
- Notify-only mode does not auto-handle events
- Notifications delivered to configured channels
- Throttling works correctly
- Filter patterns match expected events

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa watch --notify-only implemented
- [ ] Event filtering via --notify-filter
- [ ] Throttling prevents notification spam
- [ ] Desktop and webhook channels supported
- [ ] Notification includes suggested actions

 {"id":"wa-t82","title":"[EPIC] Session Recording and Replay for Debugging and Sharing","description":"# [EPIC] Session Recording and Replay

## Mission
Record wa sessions for **debugging, sharing, and analysis**—replay exactly what happened.

## Why This Matters
When something goes wrong, the #1 debugging question is: "What happened?"

Session recording provides:
- **Debugging**: Replay the sequence that led to a bug
- **Sharing**: Show others exactly what wa did
- **Analysis**: Study patterns across sessions
- **Training**: Learn from real examples

## Components

### 1. Recording Mode
Capture all events and state during a session:
```bash
$ wa watch --record
Recording session to: /path/to/.wa/recordings/2026-01-18_143201.warecord
...
^C
Session recorded: 1234 events, 45 minutes

$ ls /path/to/.wa/recordings/
2026-01-18_143201.warecord
2026-01-18_150000.warecord
```

Recording captures:
- All pane output deltas (optional, configurable)
- All detection events
- All workflow executions and step logs
- All policy decisions
- Timestamps for everything

### 2. Replay Mode
Replay a recorded session:
```bash
$ wa replay /path/to/session.warecord

Replaying session: 2026-01-18_143201
Duration: 45 minutes (1234 events)

[Press SPACE to step, ENTER to play, Q to quit]

14:32:01 > Pane 1 detected: codex.usage_limit_warning
14:32:05 > Policy check: send allowed (rate: 2/10)
14:35:12 > Pane 1 detected: codex.usage_limit_reached
14:35:12 > Workflow started: handle_usage_limits
...
```

Replay options:
- Real-time (1x speed)
- Fast-forward (10x, 100x)
- Step-by-step
- Jump to timestamp

### 3. Recording Formats
```
.warecord    - Binary format (compact, fast)
.warecord.json - JSON format (human-readable, diffable)
```

Export options:
```bash
$ wa recording export session.warecord --format json > session.json
$ wa recording export session.warecord --format markdown > session.md
```

### 4. Session Annotation
Add notes to recording:
```bash
$ wa recording annotate session.warecord --at 14:35:12 --note "This is where it went wrong"
```

### 5. Recording Sharing
Share recordings safely:
```bash
$ wa recording share session.warecord --redact
Created: session_redacted.warecord
Removed: API keys, passwords, sensitive paths
```

## Privacy Considerations
- Recordings may contain sensitive data
- Redaction mode removes known secret patterns
- Warning when recording includes sensitive paths
- Recording retention policy (auto-delete after N days)

## Storage
- Recordings stored in `.wa/recordings/`
- Configurable retention (default: 7 days)
- Compression for old recordings
- Size limits per recording

## Testing
- Unit tests: Record/replay roundtrip
- Integration tests: All event types captured
- Privacy tests: Redaction removes secrets

## Success Criteria
- Recording captures all relevant events
- Replay accurately represents recorded session
- Redaction removes sensitive information
- Recordings are portable and shareable

 {"id":"wa-thl","title":"E2E test fixtures: reproducible terminal session recordings","description":"# E2E Test Fixtures: Reproducible terminal session data

## Purpose
Pre-recorded terminal output for deterministic E2E tests.

## Implementation

### Fixture Structure
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/fixtures.rs

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct OutputChunk {
    pub delay_ms: u64,
    pub text: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct ExpectedEvent {
    pub rule_id: String,
    pub required_metadata: HashMap<String, String>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct TestFixture {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub pane_title: &'static str,
    pub agent_type: &'static str,
    pub output_chunks: Vec<OutputChunk>,
    pub expected_events: Vec<ExpectedEvent>,
}

impl TestFixture {
    pub async fn replay(&self, harness: &E2ETestHarness, pane_id: u32) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&harness.log_file, "Replaying fixture: {}", self.name);

        for chunk in &self.output_chunks {
            if chunk.delay_ms > 0 {
                tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(chunk.delay_ms)).await;
            }
            harness.inject_output(pane_id, &chunk.text).await?;
            log_debug!(&harness.log_file, "Injected: {:?}", chunk.text);
        }

        Ok(())
    }
}
```
